title: Deviation Management Done Right
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: Practical, inspection-ready deviation management for UK and EU pharma teams: risk-based triage, robust root cause, defensible impact assessment and CAPA.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury banner of a pharmaceutical quality professional in a crisp lab coat reviewing a deviation investigation on a sleek tablet at a polished laboratory bench, set against a backdrop of softly blurred stainless-steel cleanroom equipment and glass vials. Cool teal and cyan tones blend into deep navy shadows, lifted by a single subtle green accent reflecting off the glassware. Soft natural light streams from a window to the left, creating gentle highlights and a shallow depth of field that throws the background into a creamy bokeh. Composed in a 4:3 frame, elegant and precise, conveying scientific rigour and calm control. No text, no logos.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a deviation and a CAPA?
faqs.a: A deviation is a departure from an approved procedure, specification or standard that has already occurred and must be recorded and investigated. A CAPA (corrective and preventive action) is the set of actions arising from that investigation: the corrective action prevents recurrence of the specific cause, while the preventive action addresses the same risk elsewhere. One deviation may generate several CAPAs, and the deviation is only truly closed once those actions are implemented and shown to be effective.

faqs.q: How quickly must a pharmaceutical deviation be investigated?
faqs.a: There is no single regulatory number; instead, EU GMP and ICH Q9 expect timeframes proportionate to the risk and clearly defined in your SOP. A common approach is shorter targets for critical and major deviations and slightly longer ones for minor events, with documented justification for any extension. What inspectors look for is that you meet your own stated timeframes and escalate breaches, rather than allowing an open-ended backlog to build.

faqs.q: Why is 'human error' not an acceptable root cause?
faqs.a: Human error describes what happened, not why it was able to affect product, so it rarely supports an effective corrective action. A robust investigation asks why the system permitted the error to occur and reach the product, often uncovering issues with procedure design, training, workload, equipment or supervision. Closing a record with 'operator retrained' alone is a frequent inspection finding because it leaves the underlying systemic weakness untouched.
